title: Enzymes and metabolism
summary: Enzymes are the molecular machines that make life's chemistry fast enough to be useful. This lesson builds the whole of C1.1: enzymes as biological catalysts that lower activation energy, the active site and the induced-fit model, substrate specificity, and the four factors that change the rate of an enzyme-catalysed reaction — temperature, pH, substrate concentration and enzyme concentration — each with the exact shape of its graph and the reason behind it. It then covers denaturation, competitive and non-competitive inhibition, and how these reactions are organised into anabolic and catabolic metabolic pathways controlled by enzymes. Every worked example and model answer is laid out the way our marking engine actually awards Paper 2 marks, so you can see how each distinct valid point earns its mark.
learningObjectives: Explain how enzymes act as biological catalysts by lowering the activation energy of a reaction, without changing the overall energy of reactants or products.
Describe enzyme structure and the active site, and use the induced-fit model to account for substrate specificity.
Describe and explain the effect of temperature, pH, substrate concentration and enzyme concentration on the rate of an enzyme-catalysed reaction, including the shape of each graph.
Explain denaturation and distinguish competitive from non-competitive inhibition.
Define metabolism as the sum of anabolic and catabolic pathways, and explain how enzymes control those pathways.
Present explanations so that each distinct valid biological point earns a mark under analytic marking.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: The Cell's Chemical Workforce
simpleExplanation.summary: Every second, thousands of chemical reactions run inside a cell. Left to themselves most would be far too slow to keep anything alive. Enzymes are protein catalysts that speed each reaction up by lowering the energy barrier it has to cross, and because each enzyme fits only its own substrate, the cell can switch reactions on and off one by one.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a chemical reaction as pushing a boulder over a hill to roll it down the far side. The height of the hill is the activation energy — the energy needed before anything happens. An enzyme does not make the boulder heavier or the far valley deeper (it does not change the overall energy released); it digs a lower pass through the hill so the boulder gets over far more easily. And each pass is shaped for one particular boulder, so an enzyme only helps its own reaction along.
steps: A substrate molecule collides with and binds to the active site of its specific enzyme, forming an enzyme-substrate complex.
Binding makes the active site mould slightly around the substrate (induced fit), which strains the substrate's bonds and lowers the activation energy.
The reaction proceeds far faster along this lower-energy route, and the product (or products) forms.
The product leaves the active site and the unchanged enzyme is free to bind another substrate molecule and repeat — enzymes are catalysts and are not used up.

flashcards.back: A globular protein that acts as a biological catalyst, increasing the rate of a specific biochemical reaction by lowering its activation energy, without itself being changed or used up.

The minimum energy that colliding reactant molecules must have for a reaction to proceed. Enzymes LOWER this energy barrier; they do NOT change the overall energy difference ($\Delta G$) between reactants and products.
A specific region on an enzyme, formed by its three-dimensional folding, whose shape and chemistry are complementary to the substrate. This is where the substrate binds and catalysis occurs.

The active site is not rigid. When the substrate binds, it induces a conformational (shape) change in the enzyme so the active site moulds more closely around the substrate, straining its bonds and promoting the reaction.
Each enzyme catalyses only one reaction (or one type) because only a substrate with a complementary shape and chemistry can bind and induce the correct fit at the active site.

A permanent change to the three-dimensional SHAPE of an enzyme — caused by high temperature or extreme pH breaking the bonds holding the tertiary structure. The active site changes shape, the substrate can no longer bind, and activity is lost. The amino-acid SEQUENCE is unchanged.
The temperature or pH at which an enzyme's rate is fastest. Human enzymes are typically optimal near 37°C; pepsin (stomach) near pH 2, while most cell enzymes prefer near-neutral pH.

A molecule similar in shape to the substrate that binds to the ACTIVE SITE, blocking the substrate. Its effect can be OVERCOME by raising substrate concentration, so the maximum rate is still reached, just later.

A molecule that binds to a site OTHER than the active site (an allosteric site), changing the enzyme's shape so the active site no longer fits the substrate. Raising substrate concentration does NOT overcome it, so the maximum rate is lowered.
The sum of all enzyme-catalysed reactions occurring in a cell or organism, organised into metabolic pathways. It comprises anabolism and catabolism.

Anabolism BUILDS larger molecules from smaller ones and requires energy (e.g. protein synthesis, photosynthesis). Catabolism BREAKS DOWN larger molecules into smaller ones and releases energy (e.g. digestion, cell respiration).

A sequence of enzyme-catalysed reactions in which the product of one reaction is the substrate for the next. Pathways can be linear (e.g. glycolysis) or cyclical (e.g. the Krebs cycle). Each step is controlled by its own enzyme.

flashcards.front: Common trap: enzymes lower $E_a$, not $\Delta G$
flashcards.back: Enzymes lower the ACTIVATION ENERGY only. They do not supply energy, do not change whether a reaction is exergonic or endergonic, and do not alter the position of equilibrium — they simply reach it faster.

Enzymes are molecules, not organisms — they cannot die. Above the optimum they DENATURE: the tertiary structure and active-site shape change so the substrate no longer binds. Marking engines reject 'the enzyme dies' or 'the enzyme is killed'.

quickCheck.prompt: How does an enzyme increase the rate of a reaction?
options: It lowers the activation energy of the reaction
It lowers the overall free-energy change ($\Delta G$) of the reaction
It supplies energy to the reactants
It raises the temperature of the substrate
quickCheck.answer: It lowers the activation energy of the reaction

quickCheck.prompt: During denaturation of an enzyme by heat, what actually changes?
options: The sequence of amino acids in the enzyme
The three-dimensional shape of the enzyme and its active site
The identity of the substrate
The activation energy of the uncatalysed reaction
quickCheck.answer: The three-dimensional shape of the enzyme and its active site

quickCheck.prompt: A competitive inhibitor differs from a non-competitive inhibitor because it:
options: Binds to an allosteric site away from the active site
Binds to the active site and its effect can be reduced by adding more substrate
Permanently destroys the enzyme's amino-acid sequence
Lowers the maximum rate no matter how much substrate is added
quickCheck.answer: Binds to the active site and its effect can be reduced by adding more substrate

quickCheck.prompt: After catalysing a reaction, an enzyme molecule is:
options: Used up and must be replaced
Converted into product
Unchanged and free to catalyse another reaction
Permanently bound to its product
quickCheck.answer: Unchanged and free to catalyse another reaction

quickCheck.prompt: On a graph of reaction rate against substrate concentration (fixed enzyme), the curve levels off at high substrate concentration because:
options: The enzyme has denatured
All the active sites are occupied, so enzyme concentration now limits the rate
The substrate has run out
The pH has changed
quickCheck.answer: All the active sites are occupied, so enzyme concentration now limits the rate

faq.q: What is the difference between the lock-and-key model and the induced-fit model?
faq.a: The older lock-and-key model pictured a rigid active site into which only an exactly matching substrate could fit, like a key in a lock. The induced-fit model refines this: the active site is flexible, and when the substrate binds it induces a small conformational change so the enzyme moulds more closely around the substrate. This tighter fit strains the substrate's bonds and helps lower the activation energy. Induced fit explains catalysis better and is the model the syllabus expects, but it still accounts for specificity because only a substrate of roughly the right shape can trigger the correct fit.

faq.q: Why does raising the temperature eventually make an enzyme work more slowly, when higher temperature usually speeds reactions up?
faq.a: Two opposing effects are at work. Up to the optimum, raising the temperature gives molecules more kinetic energy, so substrate and enzyme collide more often and with more energy, and the rate rises. Above the optimum, the extra thermal energy starts to break the weak bonds (such as hydrogen bonds) that hold the enzyme's tertiary structure together. The active site changes shape, the substrate no longer binds, and the enzyme denatures — so the rate falls sharply. The graph therefore rises to a peak at the optimum and then drops steeply, rather than continuing to rise.

Almost all the enzymes you meet in this course are globular proteins, and for C1.1 you can assume this. There are also catalytic RNA molecules called ribozymes, which shows that catalysis is not exclusive to proteins, but the standard model of an enzyme in IB Biology is a folded globular protein with a specific active site.

faq.q: Do enzymes get used up in a reaction?
faq.a: No. This is a defining property of a catalyst. The enzyme takes part in the reaction — the substrate binds, is converted, and the product leaves — but the enzyme itself emerges unchanged and can catalyse the same reaction again and again. That is why a cell needs only tiny quantities of each enzyme. In an exam, never write that the enzyme is 'consumed' or 'used up'.

sections.content: Life runs on chemistry, but the raw chemistry of the cell is far too slow to sustain life. Breaking down a sugar or building a protein at body temperature, unassisted, could take years. Enzymes solve this: they are protein catalysts that speed up specific reactions by huge factors while working under the mild conditions inside a cell. This lesson works through what enzymes are, how the active site and induced fit give them their specificity, and how four measurable factors — temperature, pH, substrate concentration and enzyme concentration — change the rate, together with the exact shape of each graph and the reason behind it. It then covers denaturation and inhibition, and finally shows how thousands of enzyme-catalysed steps are organised into the anabolic and catabolic pathways that make up metabolism. Because C1.1 is examined with both graph interpretation and extended explanation, every worked example is set out the way marks are actually awarded — one mark for each distinct valid point.
Enzymes as biological catalysts: lowering activation energy

For any reaction to occur, the reacting molecules must collide with enough energy to break existing bonds and start forming new ones. That minimum energy is the activation energy, $E_a$ — an energy barrier that reactants must climb before they can turn into products. At the mild temperatures inside a cell, very few molecules have enough energy to cross a high barrier, so uncatalysed reactions are extremely slow. An enzyme is a biological catalyst: it provides an alternative reaction route with a LOWER activation energy, so a far greater proportion of collisions are successful and the rate increases dramatically. Crucially, the enzyme does not change the overall energy of the reactants or products — it does not make the reaction release or require more energy overall, and it does not shift the position of equilibrium. It only lowers the barrier between them, so equilibrium is reached faster. And because the enzyme is regenerated unchanged at the end, it is not used up and can act repeatedly.

items: **Activation energy ($E_a$):** the minimum energy needed for a reaction to proceed. Enzymes LOWER it.
**Catalyst:** speeds up a reaction without being changed or used up — one enzyme molecule works over and over.
**What enzymes do NOT do:** they do not change the overall energy of reactants or products ($\Delta G$), do not supply energy, and do not move the equilibrium position — only how fast it is reached.
Lowering $E_a$ means more collisions have enough energy to succeed, so the rate rises.

sections.content: Be precise: an enzyme 'increases the rate of reaction by lowering the activation energy'. Writing that it 'speeds up the reaction' or 'gives the reaction energy' will not earn the mechanism mark. The examinable idea is LOWERS ACTIVATION ENERGY — and specifically not the overall energy change of the reaction.
Enzyme structure, the active site and the induced-fit model

An enzyme is a globular protein: a long chain of amino acids folded into a precise three-dimensional shape. Within that folded shape is a small pocket or groove called the active site, whose shape and chemical properties are complementary to a particular substrate — the reactant the enzyme acts on. Only a substrate that matches the active site can bind, forming an enzyme-substrate complex, and this is why enzymes are specific: one enzyme catalyses only one reaction, or one type of reaction. The way the fit works is described by the induced-fit model. Rather than being a rigid lock waiting for its key, the active site is slightly flexible. When the correct substrate begins to bind, it induces a conformational change in the enzyme so the active site moulds more closely around the substrate. This tighter grip puts strain on the substrate's bonds, which helps lower the activation energy and makes the reaction proceed. Once the product forms and leaves, the active site returns to its original shape, ready to bind again.

items: Enzymes are **globular proteins** whose folding creates a specific **active site**.
The active site is **complementary** in shape and chemistry to one substrate → **specificity**.
**Induced fit:** substrate binding changes the active-site shape so it moulds around the substrate, straining bonds and lowering $E_a$.
Substrate + enzyme → **enzyme-substrate complex** → product + unchanged enzyme.

sections.question: Using the induced-fit model, explain why an enzyme catalyses only one type of reaction. [3]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
The enzyme's active site has a specific shape and chemistry that is complementary to only one substrate. Only that substrate can bind to the active site and induce the conformational change (induced fit) needed to form a functional enzyme-substrate complex. A molecule of a different shape cannot bind or cannot induce the correct fit, so no reaction is catalysed — making the enzyme specific to one substrate/reaction.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 3 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1:

- **Point 1:** the active site is complementary in shape (and chemistry) to only one (specific) substrate.
- **Point 2:** only that substrate can bind / induce the correct fit to form the enzyme-substrate complex.
- **Point 3:** other molecules do not fit / cannot induce the change, so are not catalysed — hence specificity.

Equivalent phrasings are accepted (e.g. 'the substrate is complementary to the active site' counts for Point 1). Repeating the same idea in two ways scores once.

sections.content: Factors affecting the rate of enzyme activity

The rate of an enzyme-catalysed reaction depends on four measurable factors: temperature, pH, substrate concentration and enzyme concentration. Each has a characteristic graph, and in the exam you are expected to draw or interpret the shape AND give the reason for it. The unifying idea is collision theory: a reaction happens when substrate molecules collide with the active sites of enzyme molecules with enough energy. Anything that changes how often, or how successfully, those collisions happen changes the rate — until some other factor becomes limiting or the enzyme is damaged.
Temperature

As temperature rises from low values, the rate increases: molecules gain kinetic energy, so enzyme and substrate move faster, collide more frequently, and more collisions have enough energy to react. The rate reaches a maximum at the optimum temperature (about 37°C for many human enzymes). Above the optimum the rate falls sharply, because the increasing thermal energy begins to break the weak bonds (such as hydrogen bonds) that hold the enzyme's tertiary structure together. The active site changes shape, the substrate can no longer bind, and the enzyme denatures. The graph is therefore a curve that rises to a peak at the optimum, then drops steeply — not a symmetrical bell, because denaturation causes a faster fall than the rise. The key contrast to make is: below the optimum, more kinetic energy → faster; above the optimum, denaturation → slower.

items: **Shape:** rises to a peak at the optimum temperature, then falls steeply.
**Below optimum:** higher temperature → more kinetic energy → more frequent, more successful collisions → faster rate.
**Above optimum:** enzyme DENATURES → active site changes shape → substrate can't bind → rate falls.
**Do not write** that the enzyme 'dies' or 'is killed' — it denatures.

sections.content: pH

Each enzyme has an optimum pH at which its rate is fastest, and moving away from that pH in either direction lowers the rate. The graph is a curve that peaks at the optimum pH and falls away on both sides. The reason is that pH affects the charges on the amino-acid side chains (R groups) that form the active site and hold the enzyme's shape. Away from the optimum, these interactions are disrupted, the active site changes shape, and the substrate binds less well; at extreme pH the enzyme denatures. Different enzymes have different optima suited to where they work — for example pepsin, in the acidic stomach, has an optimum near pH 2, whereas most enzymes inside cells work best near neutral pH.

items: **Shape:** a peak at the optimum pH, falling on both sides.
**Reason:** pH alters charges on R groups at/around the active site, changing its shape so the substrate binds less well; extreme pH denatures the enzyme.
Different enzymes have different optimum pH values matched to their environment (e.g. pepsin ≈ pH 2).

sections.content: Substrate concentration

With a fixed amount of enzyme, increasing the substrate concentration first increases the rate: more substrate molecules mean more frequent collisions with active sites, so more enzyme-substrate complexes form per second. But the curve then levels off and plateaus. At high substrate concentration the active sites become saturated — essentially all of them are occupied at any instant, so adding more substrate cannot speed things up. The rate is now limited by how fast enzymes can process substrate, i.e. by the enzyme concentration, not the substrate. The characteristic shape is therefore a curve that rises steeply, then bends over to a plateau at the maximum rate.

items: **Shape:** rises steeply, then levels off to a plateau (maximum rate).
**Rising part:** more substrate → more collisions with active sites → more enzyme-substrate complexes → faster rate.
**Plateau:** active sites are SATURATED; enzyme concentration (number of active sites) is now the limiting factor.

sections.content: Enzyme concentration

With substrate in excess, increasing the enzyme concentration increases the rate in direct proportion: more enzyme molecules mean more active sites available, so more substrate can be processed at once and more enzyme-substrate complexes form per second. The graph is a straight line through the origin — as long as there is always plenty of substrate. If substrate ever became limiting, the line would level off, because extra active sites would have no substrate to act on.

items: **Shape:** a straight line through the origin (rate directly proportional to enzyme concentration), provided substrate is in excess.
**Reason:** more enzyme → more active sites available → more enzyme-substrate complexes form per second → faster rate.
If substrate becomes limiting, the line levels off.

sections.question:
The graph below shows how the rate of an enzyme-catalysed reaction changes as temperature increases from 0°C to 60°C. The rate rises from near zero to a maximum at 40°C, then falls sharply to zero by 55°C.

(a) State the optimum temperature for this enzyme. [1]
(b) Explain the shape of the graph between 0°C and 40°C. [2]
(c) Explain the shape of the graph between 40°C and 55°C. [3]
sections.solution:
**(a)** 40°C. [1 — read directly from the peak]

**(b) Model answer (2 marks).**
As temperature increases, the enzyme and substrate molecules gain more kinetic energy and move faster, so they collide more frequently and with more energy. This means more successful collisions per second, so more enzyme-substrate complexes form and the rate increases.

*How the engine awards it — each distinct point scores 1 (max 2):*
- **Point 1:** more kinetic energy → more frequent collisions between substrate and active site.
- **Point 2:** more successful collisions / more enzyme-substrate complexes formed → higher rate.

**(c) Model answer (3 marks).**
Above the optimum, the extra thermal energy breaks the weak bonds (e.g. hydrogen bonds) holding the enzyme's tertiary structure, so the enzyme denatures. The active site changes shape and is no longer complementary to the substrate, so the substrate can no longer bind. Fewer or no enzyme-substrate complexes form, so the rate falls (to zero once all enzymes are denatured).

*How the engine awards it — each distinct point scores 1 (max 3):*
- **Point 1:** the enzyme denatures / bonds holding tertiary structure break.
- **Point 2:** the active site changes shape / is no longer complementary.
- **Point 3:** substrate can no longer bind → fewer enzyme-substrate complexes → rate falls.

*The engine rejects 'the enzyme dies' or 'is killed' for Point 1 — it requires the idea of denaturation / a shape change. Error-carried-forward applies: if a candidate states the wrong optimum in (a) but then explains the two regions correctly relative to it, the explanation marks in (b) and (c) still stand.*

sections.content: Denaturation

Denaturation is a permanent change to the three-dimensional shape of an enzyme. High temperature or extreme pH breaks the relatively weak bonds — hydrogen bonds and other interactions between R groups — that hold the folded tertiary structure in place. As the folding unravels, the active site loses its precise shape, so it is no longer complementary to the substrate; the substrate cannot bind, no enzyme-substrate complex forms, and catalytic activity is lost. The single most important exam point is what denaturation does NOT change: it does not alter the sequence of amino acids (the primary structure). Only the shape is affected. Because the bonds do not spontaneously reform correctly, denaturation is generally irreversible.

items: **Denaturation = a permanent change in the enzyme's 3-D SHAPE**, caused by heat or extreme pH.
Weak bonds holding the tertiary structure break → active site changes shape → substrate can't bind → activity lost.
The amino-acid **sequence (primary structure) is unchanged** — do not write that heat 'changes the sequence' or 'breaks the enzyme into amino acids'.
It is generally **irreversible**, and the enzyme does not 'die' — enzymes are molecules, not living things.

sections.content: Enzyme inhibition: competitive vs non-competitive

Inhibitors are molecules that reduce the rate of an enzyme-catalysed reaction, and there are two main types to contrast. A competitive inhibitor has a shape similar to the substrate, so it binds to the active site itself and blocks the substrate from entering. Because inhibitor and substrate compete for the same site, the effect depends on their relative amounts: adding more substrate makes it more likely a substrate molecule reaches the active site first, so competitive inhibition can be overcome, and the maximum rate is still reached — it just takes a higher substrate concentration to get there. A non-competitive inhibitor binds instead to a different site (an allosteric site) away from the active site. This binding changes the enzyme's overall shape, which in turn changes the shape of the active site so the substrate no longer fits well. Because the inhibitor is not competing for the active site, adding more substrate does NOT overcome it, and the maximum rate is lowered. Distinguishing the two on a graph of rate against substrate concentration: the competitive curve reaches the same plateau as the uninhibited enzyme but only at higher substrate concentration, whereas the non-competitive curve plateaus at a lower maximum rate.

items: **Competitive inhibitor:** similar shape to substrate → binds the ACTIVE SITE → blocks substrate. Overcome by adding more substrate; same maximum rate reached, just later.
**Non-competitive inhibitor:** binds an ALLOSTERIC site (not the active site) → changes enzyme/active-site shape → substrate binds poorly. NOT overcome by adding substrate; maximum rate is lowered.
**Graph tell:** competitive → same plateau at higher [substrate]; non-competitive → lower plateau.

sections.question: A student adds a competitive inhibitor to an enzyme reaction. Predict and explain the effect on the rate at (a) low substrate concentration and (b) very high substrate concentration. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
**(a) Low substrate concentration:** the rate is much lower than without the inhibitor. The inhibitor has a shape similar to the substrate, so it competes for and binds to the active site, blocking substrate molecules; with little substrate present, the inhibitor occupies many active sites, so few enzyme-substrate complexes form.
**(b) Very high substrate concentration:** the rate rises back towards the normal maximum. With a large excess of substrate, substrate molecules greatly outnumber inhibitor molecules and are far more likely to reach the active sites first, so the inhibition is effectively overcome and the maximum rate is still reached.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1:

- **Point 1 (a):** at low [substrate], rate is reduced because the inhibitor binds/blocks the active site (competes with substrate).
- **Point 2 (a):** so fewer enzyme-substrate complexes form → lower rate.
- **Point 3 (b):** at high [substrate], substrate outnumbers inhibitor / is more likely to reach the active site first.
- **Point 4 (b):** so the inhibition is overcome and the (same) maximum rate is reached.

Accept equivalents (e.g. 'substrate outcompetes the inhibitor' for Point 3). A common error the engine rejects is claiming the maximum rate is LOWERED at high substrate — that describes non-competitive, not competitive, inhibition.

sections.content: Metabolism: anabolic and catabolic pathways

Metabolism is the sum of all the enzyme-catalysed chemical reactions occurring in a cell or organism. These reactions fall into two categories. Anabolism is the building up of larger, more complex molecules from smaller ones — for example joining amino acids into proteins, or fixing carbon dioxide into glucose in photosynthesis — and it requires an input of energy (it is endergonic). Catabolism is the breaking down of larger molecules into smaller ones — for example the digestion of starch to glucose, or the oxidation of glucose in cell respiration — and it releases energy (it is exergonic). The two are linked: the energy released by catabolic reactions is used to drive anabolic ones, so metabolism as a whole keeps the cell supplied with both the molecules and the energy it needs.

items: **Metabolism:** all the enzyme-catalysed reactions in an organism.
**Anabolism:** builds larger molecules from smaller ones; requires energy (endergonic). E.g. protein synthesis, photosynthesis.
**Catabolism:** breaks larger molecules into smaller ones; releases energy (exergonic). E.g. digestion, cell respiration.
Energy from catabolism drives anabolism.

sections.content: Enzymes controlling metabolic pathways

The reactions of metabolism are not random — they are organised into metabolic pathways, sequences in which the product of one reaction becomes the substrate for the next. Every individual step is catalysed by its own specific enzyme. Because each step needs its own enzyme, the cell can control the whole pathway with great precision: by making more or less of a particular enzyme, or by switching enzymes on and off, it regulates which reactions run and how fast. Pathways can be linear, like glycolysis, with a clear start and end, or cyclical, like the Krebs cycle, where the starting molecule is regenerated each turn. Breaking a big chemical change into many small enzyme-controlled steps also lets energy be released or captured in manageable amounts, and provides many points at which the cell can regulate the flow. Enzymes are therefore not just catalysts but the control points of metabolism.

items: A **metabolic pathway** is a sequence of reactions where each product is the substrate for the next step.
**Each step has its own specific enzyme**, so the cell controls the pathway by controlling those enzymes.
Pathways can be **linear** (e.g. glycolysis) or **cyclical** (e.g. the Krebs cycle).
Small steps allow controlled energy transfer and many points of regulation.

sections.question:
A linear metabolic pathway is shown:

Substance P $\xrightarrow{\text{Enzyme 1}}$ Substance Q $\xrightarrow{\text{Enzyme 2}}$ Substance R $\xrightarrow{\text{Enzyme 3}}$ Substance S

(a) State whether the pathway would run if Enzyme 2 were non-competitively inhibited, and explain your answer. [3]
(b) Explain why the cell benefits from carrying out this conversion in three enzyme-controlled steps rather than one. [2]
sections.solution:
**(a) Model answer (3 marks).**
The pathway would stop at Substance Q. A non-competitive inhibitor binds an allosteric site on Enzyme 2 and changes the shape of its active site, so substrate Q can no longer bind and is not converted to R. Because R is the substrate for Enzyme 3, no S can be made either — the pathway is blocked from Q onwards.

*How the engine awards it — each distinct point scores 1 (max 3):*
- **Point 1:** Enzyme 2 is inhibited so Q is not converted to R (the pathway halts at Q).
- **Point 2:** the non-competitive inhibitor changes the active-site shape so substrate Q cannot bind.
- **Point 3:** R (and therefore S) cannot be produced because each product is the substrate for the next step.

**(b) Model answer (2 marks).**
Small steps allow energy to be released or used in manageable amounts rather than one large burst, and each step provides a separate control point, so the cell can regulate the pathway precisely by controlling individual enzymes.

*Each distinct point scores 1 (max 2): controlled/manageable energy transfer; multiple regulation points / finer control.*

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Saying enzymes lower $\Delta G$ or 'give the reaction energy'** — enzymes lower the ACTIVATION ENERGY only; they do not change the overall energy of reactants and products or the equilibrium position.
**Writing that a heated enzyme 'dies' or 'is killed'** — enzymes are molecules, not organisms. Above the optimum they DENATURE; the active site changes shape so the substrate can no longer bind.
**Claiming denaturation 'changes the amino-acid sequence'** — denaturation changes the SHAPE (tertiary structure); the primary sequence is unchanged.

**Confusing competitive and non-competitive inhibition** — competitive binds the active site and is overcome by more substrate (same maximum rate); non-competitive binds an allosteric site, is NOT overcome by more substrate, and lowers the maximum rate.
**Saying an enzyme is 'used up' in the reaction** — enzymes are catalysts; they emerge unchanged and act repeatedly.
**Forgetting the reason for the plateau in the substrate-concentration graph** — it levels off because active sites are SATURATED (enzyme concentration limiting), not because substrate runs out.
**Describing enzyme action vaguely as 'the substrate fits the enzyme'** — name the ACTIVE SITE and, for full credit, the induced-fit change in shape.
**Muddling anabolism and catabolism** — anabolism BUILDS UP and needs energy; catabolism BREAKS DOWN and releases energy.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

Explanation questions in C1.1 are marked analytically: each distinct valid biological point earns one mark. There is no bonus for elegant writing and no penalty for a clumsy sentence — the engine looks for named, creditable ideas. Study how the four marks below attach to four separate points, how error-carried-forward (ECF) protects a chain of reasoning, and exactly which loose phrasing the engine refuses to credit.

sections.question: Explain the effect of increasing temperature on the rate of an enzyme-catalysed reaction. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
As temperature increases towards the optimum, the enzyme and substrate molecules gain more kinetic energy and move faster, so they collide more often and more of the collisions are successful; more enzyme-substrate complexes form, so the rate increases. This continues up to an optimum temperature, at which the rate is at its maximum. Above the optimum, the increasing thermal energy breaks the bonds holding the enzyme's tertiary structure, so the enzyme denatures. The active site changes shape and is no longer complementary to the substrate, so the substrate can no longer bind and the rate falls.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1 (M):** rate increases with temperature because molecules gain kinetic energy → more frequent / more successful collisions (more enzyme-substrate complexes).
- **Point 2 (M):** the rate is maximum at an optimum temperature.
- **Point 3 (M):** above the optimum the enzyme DENATURES — bonds holding the tertiary structure break.
- **Point 4 (A):** the active site changes shape → substrate no longer binds → rate falls (the conclusion of the reasoning chain).

More than four creditable points appear here (kinetic energy, collisions, optimum, denaturation, active-site shape change, substrate can't bind), so the engine caps the award at 4 — there is deliberate room to miss one idea and still reach full marks. But marks are for DISTINCT points: writing 'the reaction goes faster because it is hotter' twice scores once.

**A for the correct step; ECF; accept equivalents.** Point 4 is the answer mark that completes the chain, and error-carried-forward applies — if a student names the wrong optimum but still explains the rise as more kinetic energy and the fall as denaturation, the explanation marks stand. Equivalent phrasings are accepted: 'the shape of the active site changes' is credited for Point 4; 'more molecules have energy above the activation energy' is credited for Point 1.

**What the engine rejects.** An answer that says the enzyme 'dies' or 'is killed' scores ZERO for Point 3, because denaturation is a change of shape, not death — enzymes are molecules. Likewise 'the enzyme melts' or 'the sequence changes' is rejected: the primary structure is unchanged. The mark scheme requires the named process (denatures → active site changes shape → substrate can't bind), not an analogy.

**Bottom line:** to secure all 4 marks, give one idea for the rise (kinetic energy / successful collisions), name the optimum, name denaturation, and finish with the active-site shape change so the substrate no longer binds. Each valid line stands on its own.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — point by point, with ECF and a cap at the marks available. So write in distinct, creditable statements: name kinetic energy and successful collisions for the rise, name the optimum, name denaturation, then state that the active site changes shape so the substrate can no longer bind. Avoid 'the enzyme dies' entirely — it scores nothing. Showing each separate point is what protects your score.
Where this leads

Enzymes underpin almost everything else in biology. The same active-site and induced-fit ideas explain how the enzymes of cell respiration release energy from glucose, how the enzymes of photosynthesis fix carbon, how DNA polymerase and other enzymes copy and repair the genome, and how digestive enzymes break down food. The rate-controlling factors you have met here — temperature, pH, substrate concentration and inhibition — return whenever biologists design experiments or model metabolism, and inhibition in particular is the basis of how many drugs and poisons work. Master the reasoning template — bind at the active site, lower the activation energy, and watch what changes the collisions or the shape — and you can explain enzyme behaviour in any context the exam sets.
